metadata.title: Prolonged fasting-induced metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle of lean and obese men
metadata.study_design: Experimental study comparing lean and obese participants during fasting periods
metadata.demographics: 18 apparently healthy young men aged from 20 to 35 years; nine lean (BMI 19–23) and nine obese (BMI 32–40 kg/m²)

evidence.quote: Prolonged fasting-induced metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle of lean and obese men
18 apparently healthy young men aged from 20 to 35 years participated; nine lean (BMI 19–23) and nine obese (BMI 32–40 kg/m²)
we examined nine obese and nine lean participants during 12 and 72 h of fasting, respectively

methods_participants.label: Muscle Biopsy Timing
methods_participants.value: Vastus lateralis of quadriceps femoris muscle

methods_participants.label: Body Composition Assessment
methods_participants.value: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry scan (QDR-2000; Hologic, USA)

methods_participants.label: Metabolomics Analysis
methods_participants.value: Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry

methods_participants.label: Blood Sampling - Daily
methods_participants.value: Daily blood samples for glucose, insulin, FFA

methods_participants.label: Adiponectin and Leptin Assay
methods_participants.value: Validated in-house time-resolved immunofluorometric assays based on commercial reagents (R&D Systems, UK)

narrative_results.statement: Prolonged fasting reduced circulating leptin levels although at a higher level in obese participants
Non-insulin-stimulated plasma free fatty acid concentrations were higher in obese during an overnight fast, but lower during prolonged fasting
Very high levels of β-OHB were found in fasting muscle with a concomitant increase in β-OHB-carnitine, demonstrating that ketone bodies were metabolized in skeletal muscle
During prolonged fasting, glycogen levels in skeletal muscle were reduced approximately 20% in both groups
There were no changes in NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD and AMP, ADP, ATP associated with prolonged fasting
Myocellular energetics were preserved and there were no suggestions of deficient substrate oxidation and obvious mitochondrial defects despite insulin resistance
In both obesity and fasting, insulin suppressed endogenous glucose production indicating that hepatic sensitivity to insulin was preserved
The metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle during acute and chronic insulin resistance reveal distinct differences
Insulin stimulation during 72 hour fasting decreased glucose oxidation and glycogen synthase phosphorylation indicating that glucose incorporation into glycogen was prioritized

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 describes total participant count (18 men: 9 lean, 9 obese), while Pass 2 only describes the obese subgroup (9 obese men). These are incompatible representations of the same fact.
Pass 1 states 'hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp' while Pass 2 states 'hyperglycemic euglycemic clamp'. These are different experimental conditions and represent a factual contradiction.

Pass 1 states 'hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp' while Pass 2 states 'hyperglycemic euglycemic clamp'. These are different experimental conditions. Also minor: 'hours' vs 'hour' but the clamp type difference is critical.

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Randomized crossover design with two study occasions separated by minimum 21 days
Hyperinsulinemic Euglycemic Clamp - Insulin Infusion Rate

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Vastus lateralis of quadriceps femoris muscle

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Vastus lateralis of quadriceps femoris muscle
Daily blood samples for glucose, insulin, FFA
Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry scan (QDR-2000; Hologic, USA)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry
Non-insulin-stimulated insulin levels were approximately 2–3 fold higher in obese than lean participants
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Non-insulin-stimulated insulin levels were approximately 2–3 fold higher in obese than lean

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Insulin levels decreased by at least 50% during 72 hours of fasting in both groups
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Insulin levels decreased by at least 50% during 72 hour fasting in both groups

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: During hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp, insulin levels were approximately 15–30% higher in obese than lean participants
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: During hyperglycemic euglycemic clamp, insulin levels were approximately 15–30% higher in obese than lean

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Prolonged fasting prompted a greater decrease in non-insulin-stimulated plasma glucose levels in lean (~35%) than obese (~25%)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Prolonged fasting prompted a greater decrease in non-insulin-stimulated plasma glucose levels in lean (approximately 35%) than obese (approximately 25%)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma free fatty acid levels were approximately 3–4 fold higher during hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp during 72 hours of fasting compared to 12 hours of fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma free fatty acid levels were approximately 3–4 fold higher during hyperglycemic euglycemic clamp during 72 hour fasting compared to 12 hour fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Despite previous observations of a higher lipolytic rate during 12 hours of fasting in obese participants, increased free fatty acid content was not found in skeletal muscle from obese participants at any time point
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Despite previous observations of a higher lipolytic rate during 12 hour fasting in obese participants, increased free fatty acid content was not found in skeletal muscle from obese participants at any timepoint

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Only modest increases in long chain fatty acids and no increases in acylcarnitines were found in skeletal muscle despite an abundant supply of long-chain fatty acids from plasma during prolonged fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Despite an abundant supply of long-chain fatty acids from plasma during prolonged fasting, only modest increases in long chain fatty acids and no increases in acylcarnitines were found in skeletal muscle

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Very high levels of β-OHB were found in fasting muscle with a concomitant increase in β-OHB-carnitine, demonstrating that ketone bodies were metabolized in skeletal muscle
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Very high levels of beta-hydroxybutyrate were found in fasting muscle with a concomitant increase in beta-hydroxybutyrate-carnitine, demonstrating that ketone bodies were metabolized in skeletal muscle

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: In obese participants, the levels of TCA-cycle intermediates citrate, succinate, and malate were reduced compared to lean independently of the duration of the fast
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: In obese participants, the levels of tricarboxylic acid cycle intermediates citrate, succinate, and malate were reduced compared to lean independently of the duration of the fast

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Acetylcarnitine in obese was lower during 12 hours of fasting, but normalized to levels similar to lean during 72 hours of fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Acetylcarnitine in obese was lower during 12 hour fasting, but normalized to levels similar to lean during 72 hour fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Endogenous glucose production was reduced during prolonged fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Endogenous glucose production, consisting of hepatic glycogenolysis and both hepatic and renal gluconeogenesis, were reduced during prolonged fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Glucose production in the non-insulin-stimulated state was lower in obese than lean during 12 hours of fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Glucose production in the non-insulin-stimulated state was lower in obese than lean during 12 hour fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The capacity for glycogenesis in the obese, measured as insulin-stimulated NOGD during 12 hours of fasting, was reduced by approximately 45%
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The capacity for glycogenesis in the obese, measured as insulin-stimulated net glucose output during 12 hour fast, was reduced by approximately 45%

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Insulin stimulation during 72 hours of fasting decreased glucose oxidation and glycogen synthase phosphorylation indicating that glucose incorporation into glycogen was prioritized
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Insulin stimulation during 72 hour fasting decreased glucose oxidation and glycogen synthase phosphorylation indicating that glucose incorporation into glycogen was prioritized

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The metabolic signature in human skeletal muscle during acute and chronic insulin resistance reveals distinct differences
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Maltotriose, a metabolite of glycogenolysis, was reduced during prolonged fasting in lean only to levels comparable to obese, suggesting that the metabolic adaptation to prolonged fasting involving changes in glycogen breakdown are lost in muscle from insulin resistant obese participants

Impaired insulin signaling in skeletal muscle may subserve insulin resistance in obesity, whereas insulin resistance associated with prolonged fasting involves other mechanisms

semantic_groups.rationale: Pass 1 describes total participant count (18 men: 9 lean, 9 obese), while Pass 2 only describes the obese subgroup (9 obese men). These are incompatible representations of the same fact.
Both extractions state identical age inclusion criteria of 20 to 35 years.
Both convey the same BMI range for lean group (19-23 kg/m2). Pass 2 includes 'BMI' label while Pass 1 omits it, but the factual content is identical.
Both convey the same BMI range for obese group (32-40 kg/m2). Pass 2 includes 'BMI' label while Pass 1 omits it, but the factual content is identical.
Both extractions state identical physical activity inclusion criteria of low levels.
Both convey the same smoking status criterion (non-smokers). Pass 1 uses 'Smoking Status' while Pass 2 uses 'Smoking' as the label, but the factual content is identical.
Both extractions state identical medication inclusion criteria of no prescription medications.
Both convey the same fasting duration of 12 hours. Pass 2 adds descriptive detail 'overnight fast' but the core fact (12 hours) is identical.
Both describe 72-hour fasting duration; pass_2 adds descriptive term 'prolonged fasting' but conveys same fact
Both report 30 mU insulin/m2/min infusion rate; pass_2 has more detailed parameter naming and expands HEC abbreviation
Both describe 2-hour HEC duration; only minor formatting and abbreviation expansion differences
Both specify ~5 mM glucose target for HEC; only parameter naming differs slightly
Both report muscle biopsy timing at t=60 and 270 minutes; pass_2 adds 'At' for clarity
Both specify approximately 100 mg sample size; capitalization and parameter naming differ slightly
Both identify vastus lateralis of quadriceps femoris as biopsy site; only parameter naming differs
Both report indirect calorimetry timing at t=180-210 and t=300-330 minutes; pass_2 adds 'At' for clarity
Both describe YSI 2300 STAT Plus glucose analyzer; pass_1 uses abbreviated 'YSI analyzer' while pass_2 uses full name. Same instrument.
Both describe ELISA for insulin assay; pass_2 adds manufacturer details (DAKO, Agilent Technologies, Denmark) which is supplementary information, not contradictory.
Identical descriptions of HbA1c assay using HPLC method.
Identical descriptions of ALAT assay using absorption photometry.
Identical descriptions of TSH assay using aluminometric assay method.
Both describe dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry for body composition; pass_1 uses 'Assay' while pass_2 uses 'Assessment' and adds device model (QDR-2000; Hologic, USA). Same underlying method.
Pass_1 contains metabolomics method information; pass_2 is empty. Information only in one extraction.
Both describe randomized crossover design; pass_2 adds specific detail about timing (minimum 21 days between occasions). Same design, pass_2 is more complete.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains participant count information. Only one side present.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains lean group participant count. Only one side present.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains blood sampling protocol details. Only one side present.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains C-peptide assay method information. Only one side present.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains cortisol assay method information. Only one side present.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains glucagon assay method information. Only one side present.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains FFA assay method information. Only one side present.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains glycerol and β-hydroxybutyrate assay method information. Only one side present.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains a complete description of adiponectin and leptin assay methodology. No comparison possible.
Both state insulin decreased by at least 50% during 72-hour fasting in both groups. Minor difference: 'of' vs omission and 'hours' vs 'hour' are negligible.
Pass 1 states 'hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp' while Pass 2 states 'hyperglycemic euglycemic clamp'. These are different experimental conditions and represent a factual contradiction.
Both convey the same fact about glucose decrease percentages in lean vs obese. Difference is only '~35%' vs 'approximately 35%' which are equivalent expressions.
Identical statements about insulin-stimulation suppressing free fatty acid levels in both groups during prolonged fasting.

Pass 1 states 'hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp' while Pass 2 states 'hyperglycemic euglycemic clamp'. These are different experimental conditions. Also minor: 'hours' vs 'hour' but the clamp type difference is critical.
Identical statements about non-insulin-stimulated plasma free fatty acid concentrations being higher in obese during overnight fast but lower during prolonged fasting.
Both convey the same fact about lipolytic rate and free fatty acid content in skeletal muscle. Differences: 'hours' vs 'hour' and 'time point' vs 'timepoint' are negligible formatting variations.
Identical statements about increased fat mass in obese being associated with higher leptin and lower adiponectin levels.
Identical text across both extractions
Same fact, only clause order differs; pass_2 reorders the introductory clause
Identical text across both extractions
Same fact; pass_2 spells out chemical abbreviations (β-OHB as beta-hydroxybutyrate) while pass_1 uses abbreviations
Identical text across both extractions
Identical text across both extractions
Same fact; pass_2 spells out TCA as tricarboxylic acid while pass_1 uses abbreviation
Same fact; pass_2 removes 'of' in '12 hour fasting' and '72 hour fasting' (minor grammatical variation)

Pass 2 provides more detailed mechanistic explanation (hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis) but both convey the same core finding that endogenous glucose production was reduced during prolonged fasting
Identical facts with only minor grammatical variation ('12 hours' vs '12 hour')
Exact match between both extractions
Same fact with minor abbreviation differences (NOGD vs 'net glucose output') and grammatical variation ('12 hours of fasting' vs '12 hour fast')
Identical facts with only minor grammatical variation ('approximately 20%' phrasing is the same)
Perfect match between both extractions
Identical facts with only minor grammatical variation ('72 hours of fasting' vs '72 hour fasting')
Same core finding with minor grammatical differences ('signature' vs 'signatures' and removal of comparison phrase)

Both describe the same finding about non-insulin-stimulated insulin levels being 2-3 fold higher in obese vs lean participants. Pass 1 includes 'participants' at the end while Pass 2 omits it, but the core fact is identical.
Pass 1 is empty while Pass 2 contains a substantive finding about maltotriose reduction during fasting. This is an orphan entry present only in Pass 2.
Pass 1 is empty while Pass 2 contains a substantive finding about impaired insulin signaling and insulin resistance mechanisms. This is an orphan entry present only in Pass 2.
